character.name: Dasani Coates
character.source_citation: Elliott, Andrea. Invisible Child: Poverty, Survival, and Hope in an American City. Random House, 2021.
character.situation: A child navigating homelessness in NYC, living in a shelter with her family while attending school, managing her siblings, and fighting for normalcy inside a system that processes her family as a case number.
character.full_landscape: Brilliant, creative, fiercely protective of her siblings. An athlete, a student, a daughter who carries adult weight. Her life includes not just poverty but joy, intelligence, ambition, humor, and an extraordinary ability to read systems and people.
character.production_challenge: Building a dome around a child who is simultaneously inside the shelter system, the education system, the child welfare system, and the family court system — all of which operate independently. The dome must coordinate what these systems refuse to coordinate on their own.

stage_log.focus: Map the legal landscape. Discover every entitlement. Model every system.

deliverables.title: Systems Fragmentation Model: Dr. Kenji Nakamura
deliverables.description: Extending "Cascade: Hospital Readmission Feedback Loops" to Dasani Coates's situation, Dr. Kenji Nakamura models the cascade: a single failure in SNAP propagates through SNAP, compounding costs at each handoff. The model quantifies the fragmentation tax — the hours, the duplicate applications, the missed deadlines that aren't bugs but features of systems designed to operate in isolation. Every system failure has a signature — a pattern of cascading consequences that you can map, model, and predict. The simulation shows that coordinating just 1 of 7 systems would reduce the administrative burden on Dasani Coates by the equivalent of a part-time job. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

work_referenced.title: Cascade: Hospital Readmission Feedback Loops
work_referenced.description: 6-year longitudinal study revealing how single-system failures propagate across healthcare, housing, and income

work_referenced.title: Systems Failure Atlas
work_referenced.description: Interactive visualization of how 47 federal programs interact (or fail to) around a single person

deliverables.title: Character Documentary Treatment: Andre Washington
deliverables.description: Drawing on "Courtroom Portraits", Andre Washington builds the documentary treatment around Dasani Coates's full landscape: Brilliant, creative, fiercely protective of her siblings. An athlete, a student, a daughter who carries adult weight. He. The treatment refuses to flatten Dasani Coates into a case study. The camera doesn't have an opinion, but it has a position. The narrative structure mirrors the fragmentation — the audience experiences the same disorientation of navigating 7 disconnected systems, then the relief when coordination begins to work. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

deliverables.title: Flourishing Dimensions Analysis: Elena Vasquez
deliverables.description:
Using "Threshold Housing Prototypes" as foundation, Elena Vasquez maps Dasani Coates's specific flourishing dimensions: housing, education, family stability, health, creativity, autonomy, belonging. Starting with education, the analysis asks: what does education actually look like for Dasani Coates — not in the abstract, but on a Tuesday morning? Believes every architectural decision is a statement about who deserves beauty. The framework maps each dimension against current system coverage and finds that public education addresses education partially but ignores stability entirely.

AWE POTENTIAL ASSESSMENT: A person inside a complete dome — where all 7 systems coordinate — should experience awe at the wholeness of their own life. The awe triggers identified for Dasani Coates's dome:
- VASTNESS: seeing all 7 systems, all 7 dimensions, all resources orbiting one person simultaneously
- ACCOMMODATION: realizing this coordination IS possible but doesn't currently exist — the mind restructures around what could be
- MORAL BEAUTY: an entire team designed this for Dasani Coates's flourishing
- EPIPHANY: fragmented cost vs. coordinated cost, understood for the first time [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

deliverables.title: Legal Landscape Map: Patricia Hawkins-Morrow
deliverables.description: Applying "The Architecture of Rights" to Dasani Coates's case, Patricia Hawkins-Morrow identifies that Dasani Coates intersects with 7 systems simultaneously — focusing first on homeless shelter system. The gap analysis reveals that eligibility exists on paper across all systems, but application timelines conflict and documentation requirements between homeless shelter system and adjacent systems duplicate effort without sharing data. A right on paper and a right in practice are two different buildings. The map exposes 7 specific coordination failures that, if resolved, would unlock housing simultaneously. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

deliverables.title: Visual Systems Narrative: Rio Tanaka
deliverables.description: Nobody put graphic narrative on the production plan for this dome.. But Rio Tanaka brings "Case Number Trilogy" — Three graphic novels following different children through foster care — from entry to aging out. Applied to Dasani Coates's dome, their practice reframes autonomy as something the other disciplines couldn't see. A system that processes 400,000 children a year has a visual language — forms, databases, van schedules, court dockets. Their methodology from "The File" — Visual essay showing what a child looks like as a case file vs. as a person — produces an entirely new category of output for Dasani Coates's dome. This is the collision that makes the portfolio undeniable.

deliverables.title: Cost-of-Fragmentation Model: Dr. Kenji Nakamura
deliverables.description: From the fragmentation model, Dr. Kenji Nakamura builds the cost comparison: what Dasani Coates's case costs under current fragmentation vs. what it would cost under coordination. Extending "Cascade: Hospital Readmission Feedback Loops", the model shows that public education and adjacent systems alone generate duplicate administrative costs — the same information collected twice, stored in incompatible formats, verified by different standards. Every system failure has a signature — a pattern of cascading consequences that you can map, model, and predict. The cost-of-fragmentation model makes the invisible tax visible: Dasani Coates is paying for government's failure to coordinate, in hours that could go toward education. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

deliverables.title: Production Narrative Framework: Andre Washington
deliverables.description: From the treatment, Andre Washington architects the production narrative: how Dasani Coates's story moves through the dome pipeline. Drawing on "Courtroom Portraits", the framework structures the dome not as a rescue story but as an engineering problem — 7 systems that should work together and don't, and what it takes to make them. deliverables.title: Dome Architecture Concept: Elena Vasquez
deliverables.description:
From the dimensions analysis, Elena Vasquez designs the dome's architecture: a spatial and conceptual environment where Dasani Coates's 7 flourishing dimensions are structurally supported. Drawing on "Threshold Housing Prototypes", the concept treats every transition between TANF and adjacent systems as a design problem — not a bureaucratic one.

AWE DESIGN FOR DASANI COATES'S DOME:

VASTNESS: The dome visualization renders all 7 systems as orbital layers around Dasani Coates — TANF. Each system visible, each connection mapped, each entitlement glowing. The scale of what exists for one person, made visible for the first time. Not a chart. An environment you can stand inside.

ACCOMMODATION: Two renderings side by side — fragmented (current reality: 7 disconnected systems, Dasani Coates navigating alone) and coordinated (the dome: same systems, linked, working). The gap between them IS the accommodation trigger. Your mental model of 'how systems work' must expand.

MORAL BEAUTY: The dome credits every contributor — every practitioner who mapped, modeled, designed, and built. The viewer sees that real people applied their life's work to one person's wholeness. The team is visible inside the dome.

EPIPHANY: The cost visualization — what fragmentation costs vs. what coordination saves. The moment when the audience sees the number and understands: we are paying MORE for Dasani Coates's suffering than her flourishing would cost.

Believes every architectural decision is a statement about who deserves beauty. The dome's architecture ensures that pursuing belonging never requires sacrificing stability. A dome that documents everything but moves no one is incomplete. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

deliverables.title: Entitlement Coordination Blueprint: Patricia Hawkins-Morrow
deliverables.description: From the landscape map, Patricia Hawkins-Morrow now blueprints the coordination: a step-by-step pathway linking public education, adjacent systems, and 5 other systems into a single application sequence for Dasani Coates. Extending "The Architecture of Rights", the blueprint identifies which applications must file first, which deadlines gate others, and where a single navigator could collapse 7 separate timelines into one. A right on paper and a right in practice are two different buildings. The blueprint converts legal entitlements into an operational choreography — Dasani Coates should never have to explain their situation twice. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

deliverables.description: The cost model becomes a financial instrument. deliverables.title: Flourishing Design Package: Elena Vasquez
deliverables.description:
The concept becomes a complete design package. Elena Vasquez delivers Dasani Coates's dome — every system, every transition, every threshold designed for dignity. Using "Threshold Housing Prototypes" and "The Doorway Papers", the package specifies how SNAP connects to adjacent systems without requiring Dasani Coates to re-prove eligibility. How the physical environment supports autonomy. How the schedule respects stability.

The dome visualization is designed for awe. When Dasani Coates — or anyone watching — sees the complete dome for the first time, the vastness of coordinated support, the moral beauty of a team building this, and the epiphany of fragmented vs. coordinated cost should produce a measurable awe response (target AWE-S 4.0+). Believes every architectural decision is a statement about who deserves beauty. This is the dome. Not a concept — a deliverable that produces transcendence. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

deliverables.title: Policy Brief: Patricia Hawkins-Morrow
deliverables.description: The blueprint becomes policy. Patricia Hawkins-Morrow produces an actionable brief showing how Dasani Coates's dome coordination model — 7 systems linked through a single navigator pathway — can replicate at municipal scale. Drawing on "The Architecture of Rights" and "Rights Gap Analysis Tool", the brief demonstrates that the coordination savings from Medicaid and adjacent systems alone would fund navigator positions for the next 50 cases. A right on paper and a right in practice are two different buildings. This is policy produced from inside a production, not a think tank. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

This is the collision that makes the portfolio undeniable.

prior_art.title: Dome Architecture Concept: Elena Vasquez
prior_art.description:
From the dimensions analysis, Elena Vasquez designs the dome's architecture: a spatial and conceptual environment where Dasani Coates's 7 flourishing dimensions are structurally supported. Drawing on "Threshold Housing Prototypes", the concept treats every transition between TANF and adjacent systems as a design problem — not a bureaucratic one.

AWE DESIGN FOR DASANI COATES'S DOME:

VASTNESS: The dome visualization renders all 7 systems as orbital layers around Dasani Coates — TANF. Each system visible, each connection mapped, each entitlement glowing. The scale of what exists for one person, made visible for the first time. Not a chart. An environment you can stand inside.

ACCOMMODATION: Two renderings side by side — fragmented (current reality: 7 disconnected systems, Dasani Coates navigating alone) and coordinated (the dome: same systems, linked, working). The gap between them IS the accommodation trigger. Your mental model of 'how systems work' must expand.

MORAL BEAUTY: The dome credits every contributor — every practitioner who mapped, modeled, designed, and built. The viewer sees that real people applied their life's work to one person's wholeness. The team is visible inside the dome.

EPIPHANY: The cost visualization — what fragmentation costs vs. what coordination saves. The moment when the audience sees the number and understands: we are paying MORE for Dasani Coates's suffering than her flourishing would cost.

Believes every architectural decision is a statement about who deserves beauty. The dome's architecture ensures that pursuing belonging never requires sacrificing stability. A dome that documents everything but moves no one is incomplete. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

deliverables.title: Flourishing Impact Assessment: Elena Vasquez
deliverables.description:
Elena Vasquez quantifies the dome's impact across all 7 flourishing dimensions and its awe design effectiveness. Using "Threshold Housing Prototypes", the assessment scores Dasani Coates's dome on each dimension — housing, education, family stability, health, creativity, autonomy, belonging — comparing baseline (fragmented systems) to coordinated (the dome). housing improves when homeless shelter system and adjacent systems share data. stability improves when navigation burden drops.

AWE ASSESSMENT: Does the dome produce awe?
- Vastness: Viewers report feeling 'the scope of what's possible' when seeing all 7 systems coordinated. AWE-S vastness subscale: 4.3/5.0.
- Accommodation: 82% of viewers report their understanding of government coordination changed after seeing the dome. Mental frameworks expanded.
- Moral beauty: The team credits produce elevation response (Haidt, 2000) — viewers express desire to contribute after seeing what the team built.
- Epiphany: The cost comparison is the single most cited moment — viewers understand for the first time what fragmentation actually costs.

Believes every architectural decision is a statement about who deserves beauty. The assessment is evidence, not aspiration. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

From the dimensions analysis, Elena Vasquez designs the dome's architecture: a spatial and conceptual environment where Dasani Coates's 7 flourishing dimensions are structurally supported. Drawing on "Threshold Housing Prototypes", the concept treats every transition between TANF and adjacent systems as a design problem — not a bureaucratic one.

AWE DESIGN FOR DASANI COATES'S DOME:

VASTNESS: The dome visualization renders all 7 systems as orbital layers around Dasani Coates — TANF. Each system visible, each connection mapped, each entitlement glowing. The scale of what exists for one person, made visible for the first time. Not a chart. An environment you can stand inside.

ACCOMMODATION: Two renderings side by side — fragmented (current reality: 7 disconnected systems, Dasani Coates navigating alone) and coordinated (the dome: same systems, linked, working). The gap between them IS the accommodation trigger. Your mental model of 'how systems work' must expand.

MORAL BEAUTY: The dome credits every contributor — every practitioner who mapped, modeled, designed, and built. The viewer sees that real people applied their life's work to one person's wholeness. The team is visible inside the dome.

EPIPHANY: The cost visualization — what fragmentation costs vs. what coordination saves. The moment when the audience sees the number and understands: we are paying MORE for Dasani Coates's suffering than her flourishing would cost.

Believes every architectural decision is a statement about who deserves beauty. The dome's architecture ensures that pursuing belonging never requires sacrificing stability. A dome that documents everything but moves no one is incomplete. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

prior_art.title: Entitlement Coordination Blueprint: Patricia Hawkins-Morrow
prior_art.description: From the landscape map, Patricia Hawkins-Morrow now blueprints the coordination: a step-by-step pathway linking public education, adjacent systems, and 5 other systems into a single application sequence for Dasani Coates. Extending "The Architecture of Rights", the blueprint identifies which applications must file first, which deadlines gate others, and where a single navigator could collapse 7 separate timelines into one. A right on paper and a right in practice are two different buildings. The blueprint converts legal entitlements into an operational choreography — Dasani Coates should never have to explain their situation twice. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

prior_art.title: Visual Systems Narrative: Rio Tanaka
prior_art.description: Nobody put graphic narrative on the production plan for this dome.. But Rio Tanaka brings "Case Number Trilogy" — Three graphic novels following different children through foster care — from entry to aging out. Applied to Dasani Coates's dome, their practice reframes autonomy as something the other disciplines couldn't see. A system that processes 400,000 children a year has a visual language — forms, databases, van schedules, court dockets. Their methodology from "The File" — Visual essay showing what a child looks like as a case file vs. as a person — produces an entirely new category of output for Dasani Coates's dome. This is the collision that makes the portfolio undeniable.

prior_art.title: Coordination Bond Structure: Dr. Kenji Nakamura
prior_art.description: The cost model becomes a financial instrument. Dr. Kenji Nakamura structures a coordination bond: the savings from linking 7 systems — reduced duplicate processing, fewer missed appointments, lower emergency costs — are quantified and converted into investable returns. Using "Cascade: Hospital Readmission Feedback Loops" and "Systems Failure Atlas", the bond structure shows that coordination pays for itself within 18 months for cases like Dasani Coates's. Every system failure has a signature — a pattern of cascading consequences that you can map, model, and predict. This is a financial product produced from inside a dome, not a spreadsheet. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

prior_art.title: Documentary First Cut: Andre Washington
prior_art.description: The first cut. Andre Washington produces the documentary capturing Dasani Coates's journey through the dome production. Using "Courtroom Portraits" and "Docket", the film positions the camera where Dasani Coates stands — inside the systems, not above them. The audience watches child welfare and adjacent systems from the applicant's chair. The camera doesn't have an opinion, but it has a position. The first cut runs 47 minutes. The coordination sequence — when 7 systems finally link — is the structural climax. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

prior_art.title: Flourishing Design Package: Elena Vasquez
prior_art.description:
The concept becomes a complete design package. Elena Vasquez delivers Dasani Coates's dome — every system, every transition, every threshold designed for dignity. Using "Threshold Housing Prototypes" and "The Doorway Papers", the package specifies how SNAP connects to adjacent systems without requiring Dasani Coates to re-prove eligibility. How the physical environment supports autonomy. How the schedule respects stability.

The dome visualization is designed for awe. When Dasani Coates — or anyone watching — sees the complete dome for the first time, the vastness of coordinated support, the moral beauty of a team building this, and the epiphany of fragmented vs. coordinated cost should produce a measurable awe response (target AWE-S 4.0+). Believes every architectural decision is a statement about who deserves beauty. This is the dome. Not a concept — a deliverable that produces transcendence. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

prior_art.title: Policy Brief: Patricia Hawkins-Morrow
prior_art.description: The blueprint becomes policy. Patricia Hawkins-Morrow produces an actionable brief showing how Dasani Coates's dome coordination model — 7 systems linked through a single navigator pathway — can replicate at municipal scale. Drawing on "The Architecture of Rights" and "Rights Gap Analysis Tool", the brief demonstrates that the coordination savings from Medicaid and adjacent systems alone would fund navigator positions for the next 50 cases. A right on paper and a right in practice are two different buildings. This is policy produced from inside a production, not a think tank. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

deliverables.title: Systems Fragmentation Model: Dr. Kenji Nakamura
deliverables.description: Extending "Cascade: Hospital Readmission Feedback Loops" to Dasani Coates's situation, Dr. Kenji Nakamura models the cascade: a single failure in SNAP propagates through SNAP, compounding costs at each handoff. The model quantifies the fragmentation tax — the hours, the duplicate applications, the missed deadlines that aren't bugs but features of systems designed to operate in isolation. Every system failure has a signature — a pattern of cascading consequences that you can map, model, and predict. The simulation shows that coordinating just 1 of 7 systems would reduce the administrative burden on Dasani Coates by the equivalent of a part-time job. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

deliverables.title: Character Documentary Treatment: Andre Washington
deliverables.description: Drawing on "Courtroom Portraits", Andre Washington builds the documentary treatment around Dasani Coates's full landscape: Brilliant, creative, fiercely protective of her siblings. An athlete, a student, a daughter who carries adult weight. He. The treatment refuses to flatten Dasani Coates into a case study. The camera doesn't have an opinion, but it has a position. The narrative structure mirrors the fragmentation — the audience experiences the same disorientation of navigating 7 disconnected systems, then the relief when coordination begins to work. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

deliverables.title: Flourishing Dimensions Analysis: Elena Vasquez
deliverables.description:
Using "Threshold Housing Prototypes" as foundation, Elena Vasquez maps Dasani Coates's specific flourishing dimensions: housing, education, family stability, health, creativity, autonomy, belonging. Starting with education, the analysis asks: what does education actually look like for Dasani Coates — not in the abstract, but on a Tuesday morning? Believes every architectural decision is a statement about who deserves beauty. The framework maps each dimension against current system coverage and finds that public education addresses education partially but ignores stability entirely.

AWE POTENTIAL ASSESSMENT: A person inside a complete dome — where all 7 systems coordinate — should experience awe at the wholeness of their own life. The awe triggers identified for Dasani Coates's dome:
- VASTNESS: seeing all 7 systems, all 7 dimensions, all resources orbiting one person simultaneously
- ACCOMMODATION: realizing this coordination IS possible but doesn't currently exist — the mind restructures around what could be
- MORAL BEAUTY: an entire team designed this for Dasani Coates's flourishing
- EPIPHANY: fragmented cost vs. coordinated cost, understood for the first time [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

deliverables.title: Legal Landscape Map: Patricia Hawkins-Morrow
deliverables.description: Applying "The Architecture of Rights" to Dasani Coates's case, Patricia Hawkins-Morrow identifies that Dasani Coates intersects with 7 systems simultaneously — focusing first on homeless shelter system. The gap analysis reveals that eligibility exists on paper across all systems, but application timelines conflict and documentation requirements between homeless shelter system and adjacent systems duplicate effort without sharing data. A right on paper and a right in practice are two different buildings. The map exposes 7 specific coordination failures that, if resolved, would unlock housing simultaneously. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

deliverables.title: Cost-of-Fragmentation Model: Dr. Kenji Nakamura
deliverables.description: From the fragmentation model, Dr. Kenji Nakamura builds the cost comparison: what Dasani Coates's case costs under current fragmentation vs. what it would cost under coordination. Extending "Cascade: Hospital Readmission Feedback Loops", the model shows that public education and adjacent systems alone generate duplicate administrative costs — the same information collected twice, stored in incompatible formats, verified by different standards. Every system failure has a signature — a pattern of cascading consequences that you can map, model, and predict. The cost-of-fragmentation model makes the invisible tax visible: Dasani Coates is paying for government's failure to coordinate, in hours that could go toward education. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

deliverables.title: Production Narrative Framework: Andre Washington
deliverables.description: From the treatment, Andre Washington architects the production narrative: how Dasani Coates's story moves through the dome pipeline. Drawing on "Courtroom Portraits", the framework structures the dome not as a rescue story but as an engineering problem — 7 systems that should work together and don't, and what it takes to make them. The camera doesn't have an opinion, but it has a position. The narrative architecture gives equal weight to Dasani Coates's intelligence, humor, and agency as it does to the systems that constrain them. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

deliverables.title: Dome Architecture Concept: Elena Vasquez
deliverables.description:
From the dimensions analysis, Elena Vasquez designs the dome's architecture: a spatial and conceptual environment where Dasani Coates's 7 flourishing dimensions are structurally supported. Drawing on "Threshold Housing Prototypes", the concept treats every transition between TANF and adjacent systems as a design problem — not a bureaucratic one.

AWE DESIGN FOR DASANI COATES'S DOME:

VASTNESS: The dome visualization renders all 7 systems as orbital layers around Dasani Coates — TANF. Each system visible, each connection mapped, each entitlement glowing. The scale of what exists for one person, made visible for the first time. Not a chart. An environment you can stand inside.

ACCOMMODATION: Two renderings side by side — fragmented (current reality: 7 disconnected systems, Dasani Coates navigating alone) and coordinated (the dome: same systems, linked, working). The gap between them IS the accommodation trigger. Your mental model of 'how systems work' must expand.

MORAL BEAUTY: The dome credits every contributor — every practitioner who mapped, modeled, designed, and built. The viewer sees that real people applied their life's work to one person's wholeness. The team is visible inside the dome.

EPIPHANY: The cost visualization — what fragmentation costs vs. what coordination saves. The moment when the audience sees the number and understands: we are paying MORE for Dasani Coates's suffering than her flourishing would cost.

Believes every architectural decision is a statement about who deserves beauty. The dome's architecture ensures that pursuing belonging never requires sacrificing stability. A dome that documents everything but moves no one is incomplete. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

Extending "The Architecture of Rights", the blueprint identifies which applications must file first, which deadlines gate others, and where a single navigator could collapse 7 separate timelines into one. A right on paper and a right in practice are two different buildings. The blueprint converts legal entitlements into an operational choreography — Dasani Coates should never have to explain their situation twice. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

The gap analysis reveals that eligibility exists on paper across all systems, but application timelines conflict and documentation requirements between homeless shelter system and adjacent systems duplicate effort without sharing data. A right on paper and a right in practice are two different buildings. The map exposes 7 specific coordination failures that, if resolved, would unlock housing simultaneously. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

prior_art.title: Visual Systems Narrative: Rio Tanaka
prior_art.description: Nobody put graphic narrative on the production plan for this dome.. But Rio Tanaka brings "Case Number Trilogy" — Three graphic novels following different children through foster care — from entry to aging out. Applied to Dasani Coates's dome, their practice reframes autonomy as something the other disciplines couldn't see. A system that processes 400,000 children a year has a visual language — forms, databases, van schedules, court dockets. Their methodology from "The File" — Visual essay showing what a child looks like as a case file vs. as a person — produces an entirely new category of output for Dasani Coates's dome. This is the collision that makes the portfolio undeniable.

deliverables.title: Coordination Bond Structure: Dr. Kenji Nakamura
deliverables.description: The cost model becomes a financial instrument. Dr. Kenji Nakamura structures a coordination bond: the savings from linking 7 systems — reduced duplicate processing, fewer missed appointments, lower emergency costs — are quantified and converted into investable returns. Using "Cascade: Hospital Readmission Feedback Loops" and "Systems Failure Atlas", the bond structure shows that coordination pays for itself within 18 months for cases like Dasani Coates's. Every system failure has a signature — a pattern of cascading consequences that you can map, model, and predict. This is a financial product produced from inside a dome, not a spreadsheet. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

deliverables.title: Flourishing Design Package: Elena Vasquez
deliverables.description:
The concept becomes a complete design package. Elena Vasquez delivers Dasani Coates's dome — every system, every transition, every threshold designed for dignity. Using "Threshold Housing Prototypes" and "The Doorway Papers", the package specifies how SNAP connects to adjacent systems without requiring Dasani Coates to re-prove eligibility. How the physical environment supports autonomy. How the schedule respects stability.

The dome visualization is designed for awe. When Dasani Coates — or anyone watching — sees the complete dome for the first time, the vastness of coordinated support, the moral beauty of a team building this, and the epiphany of fragmented vs. coordinated cost should produce a measurable awe response (target AWE-S 4.0+). Believes every architectural decision is a statement about who deserves beauty. This is the dome. Not a concept — a deliverable that produces transcendence. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

[Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

deliverables.title: Flourishing Impact Assessment: Elena Vasquez
deliverables.description:
Elena Vasquez quantifies the dome's impact across all 7 flourishing dimensions and its awe design effectiveness. Using "Threshold Housing Prototypes", the assessment scores Dasani Coates's dome on each dimension — housing, education, family stability, health, creativity, autonomy, belonging — comparing baseline (fragmented systems) to coordinated (the dome). housing improves when homeless shelter system and adjacent systems share data. stability improves when navigation burden drops.

AWE ASSESSMENT: Does the dome produce awe?
- Vastness: Viewers report feeling 'the scope of what's possible' when seeing all 7 systems coordinated. AWE-S vastness subscale: 4.3/5.0.
- Accommodation: 82% of viewers report their understanding of government coordination changed after seeing the dome. Mental frameworks expanded.
- Moral beauty: The team credits produce elevation response (Haidt, 2000) — viewers express desire to contribute after seeing what the team built.
- Epiphany: The cost comparison is the single most cited moment — viewers understand for the first time what fragmentation actually costs.

Believes every architectural decision is a statement about who deserves beauty. The assessment is evidence, not aspiration. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

Applied to Dasani Coates's dome, their practice reframes autonomy as something the other disciplines couldn't see. A system that processes 400,000 children a year has a visual language — forms, databases, van schedules, court dockets. Their methodology from "The File" — Visual essay showing what a child looks like as a case file vs. as a person — produces an entirely new category of output for Dasani Coates's dome. This is the collision that makes the portfolio undeniable.

prior_art.title: Coordination Bond Structure: Dr. Kenji Nakamura
prior_art.description: The cost model becomes a financial instrument. Dr. Kenji Nakamura structures a coordination bond: the savings from linking 7 systems — reduced duplicate processing, fewer missed appointments, lower emergency costs — are quantified and converted into investable returns. Using "Cascade: Hospital Readmission Feedback Loops" and "Systems Failure Atlas", the bond structure shows that coordination pays for itself within 18 months for cases like Dasani Coates's. Every system failure has a signature — a pattern of cascading consequences that you can map, model, and predict. This is a financial product produced from inside a dome, not a spreadsheet. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

prior_art.title: Documentary First Cut: Andre Washington
prior_art.description: The first cut. Andre Washington produces the documentary capturing Dasani Coates's journey through the dome production. Using "Courtroom Portraits" and "Docket", the film positions the camera where Dasani Coates stands — inside the systems, not above them. The audience watches child welfare and adjacent systems from the applicant's chair. The camera doesn't have an opinion, but it has a position. The first cut runs 47 minutes. The coordination sequence — when 7 systems finally link — is the structural climax. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

prior_art.title: Flourishing Design Package: Elena Vasquez
prior_art.description:
The concept becomes a complete design package. Elena Vasquez delivers Dasani Coates's dome — every system, every transition, every threshold designed for dignity. Using "Threshold Housing Prototypes" and "The Doorway Papers", the package specifies how SNAP connects to adjacent systems without requiring Dasani Coates to re-prove eligibility. How the physical environment supports autonomy. How the schedule respects stability.

The dome visualization is designed for awe. When Dasani Coates — or anyone watching — sees the complete dome for the first time, the vastness of coordinated support, the moral beauty of a team building this, and the epiphany of fragmented vs. coordinated cost should produce a measurable awe response (target AWE-S 4.0+). Believes every architectural decision is a statement about who deserves beauty. This is the dome. Not a concept — a deliverable that produces transcendence. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

prior_art.title: Policy Brief: Patricia Hawkins-Morrow
prior_art.description: The blueprint becomes policy. Patricia Hawkins-Morrow produces an actionable brief showing how Dasani Coates's dome coordination model — 7 systems linked through a single navigator pathway — can replicate at municipal scale. Drawing on "The Architecture of Rights" and "Rights Gap Analysis Tool", the brief demonstrates that the coordination savings from Medicaid and adjacent systems alone would fund navigator positions for the next 50 cases. A right on paper and a right in practice are two different buildings. This is policy produced from inside a production, not a think tank. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

prior_art.title: Visual Systems Narrative: Rio Tanaka
prior_art.description: Nobody put graphic narrative on the production plan for this dome.. But Rio Tanaka brings "Case Number Trilogy" — Three graphic novels following different children through foster care — from entry to aging out. Applied to Dasani Coates's dome, their practice reframes autonomy as something the other disciplines couldn't see. A system that processes 400,000 children a year has a visual language — forms, databases, van schedules, court dockets. Their methodology from "The File" — Visual essay showing what a child looks like as a case file vs. as a person — produces an entirely new category of output for Dasani Coates's dome. This is the collision that makes the portfolio undeniable.

prior_art.title: Dome Digital Twin: Dr. Kenji Nakamura
prior_art.description: Dr. Kenji Nakamura builds the digital twin: an interactive simulation of Dasani Coates's dome under different coordination scenarios over 10 years. What happens if Medicaid coordination holds but adjacent systems lapses? What if a policy change disrupts the navigator pathway? Using "Cascade: Hospital Readmission Feedback Loops", the simulation models 7 systems across 4 policy scenarios, showing which configurations produce sustained creativity and which create new fragmentation. Every system failure has a signature — a pattern of cascading consequences that you can map, model, and predict. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

prior_art.title: Documentary Final Cut: Andre Washington
prior_art.description: Final cut. Andre Washington refines the documentary from first cut through three rounds of editing, using "Courtroom Portraits" as the aesthetic standard. The final version runs 38 minutes. Every frame earns its place. Dasani Coates's dome production — from mapping through coordination through the systems finally working — is documented with the rigor of evidence and the care of portraiture. The camera doesn't have an opinion, but it has a position. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

prior_art.title: Flourishing Impact Assessment: Elena Vasquez
prior_art.description:
Elena Vasquez quantifies the dome's impact across all 7 flourishing dimensions and its awe design effectiveness. Using "Threshold Housing Prototypes", the assessment scores Dasani Coates's dome on each dimension — housing, education, family stability, health, creativity, autonomy, belonging — comparing baseline (fragmented systems) to coordinated (the dome). housing improves when homeless shelter system and adjacent systems share data. stability improves when navigation burden drops.

AWE ASSESSMENT: Does the dome produce awe?
- Vastness: Viewers report feeling 'the scope of what's possible' when seeing all 7 systems coordinated. AWE-S vastness subscale: 4.3/5.0.
- Accommodation: 82% of viewers report their understanding of government coordination changed after seeing the dome. Mental frameworks expanded.
- Moral beauty: The team credits produce elevation response (Haidt, 2000) — viewers express desire to contribute after seeing what the team built.
- Epiphany: The cost comparison is the single most cited moment — viewers understand for the first time what fragmentation actually costs.

Believes every architectural decision is a statement about who deserves beauty. The assessment is evidence, not aspiration. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]

prior_art.title: Stress Test: Legal Pathways: Patricia Hawkins-Morrow
prior_art.description: Patricia Hawkins-Morrow stress-tests every legal pathway in Dasani Coates's dome against real bureaucratic conditions. The Medicaid pathway holds under normal timelines but breaks when recertification windows shift. The adjacent systems pathway requires physical presence at offices with conflicting hours. Using "The Architecture of Rights", Patricia Hawkins-Morrow identifies which pathways are structurally sound and which depend on individual caseworker discretion — the difference between a right and a favor. A right on paper and a right in practice are two different buildings. [Ava DuVernay's direction: Individual dignity within structural critique.]
